title: Whakapapa of the River: Voices of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Kōrero o te Awa: Ngā Reo o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the oral histories and written narratives surrounding Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway for Ngāti Kahungunu that flows through our rohe. This stream holds stories of ancestral journeys, traditional food gathering practices, and environmental changes. The lesson connects to local kaumātua knowledge about the stream's historical significance and current restoration efforts led by our community.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare oral histories and written texts about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to identify narrative techniques and cultural perspectives
Create a written piece (narrative or report) that synthesizes community voices with personal observations about the awa
Use appropriate language features and structures to communicate findings about the stream's significance to different audiences
Participate effectively in group discussions by building on others' ideas and respecting diverse viewpoints about local environmental issues

activities.title: Whakarongo: Listening to the Awa's Stories
activities.description: Students listen to recorded oral histories from local kaumātua about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, then work in pairs to identify key themes, narrative structures, and language features used in these traditional accounts

activities.title: Tuhituhi: Mapping Narrative Journeys
activities.description: Using large maps of the awa, students create visual representations of the stories they've heard, annotating geographical features with corresponding narrative elements and cultural significance markers

activities.title: Uiui: Community Voice Gathering
activities.description: Students develop interview questions and conduct structured interviews with local community members (restoration group members, farmers, historians) about their relationships with the awa, practicing active listening and note-taking techniques

activities.title: Whakamārama: Synthesizing Multiple Perspectives
activities.description: In small groups, students analyze their collected data to identify common themes, contrasting viewpoints, and evolving narratives about the awa, using graphic organizers to compare oral and written accounts

activities.title: Whakaputa: Creating Our Awa Narrative
activities.description: Students craft a multi-format presentation (written report, digital story, or spoken word piece) that weaves together traditional stories, community voices, and personal reflections to tell a comprehensive story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua oral history recordings from Hastings District Council archives
Physical and digital maps of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku watershed from Hawke's Bay Regional Council
Mangakōtukutuku Stream Care Group members as expert interviewees
'Te Awa Atua: The River of Life' by Anne Salmond (relevant chapters)

tasks: Written synthesis report analyzing multiple perspectives about the awa
Recorded oral presentation sharing findings with authentic audience
Reflective journal documenting the interview and research process

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Ideas; Language features; Structure
English: Purposes and audiences - Show understanding of how texts are shaped for different purposes and audiences
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways
